name: Linear
tagline: Issue tracking and project management for high-performance teams.
description: Linear is a streamlined issue tracking and project management platform designed specifically for software teams that value speed and efficiency. With its keyboard-first interface and sophisticated automation engine, Linear helps engineering teams plan, track, and ship software faster while reducing administrative overhead.
pros: Sub-100ms search across 10,000+ issues with instant filtering by status, assignee, label, priority, and cycle — no loading spinners or pagination delays
Keyboard-first design with 200+ shortcuts lets experienced users manage their entire workflow without touching a mouse, reducing context-switching friction
Onboarding from signup to active issue creation takes under 5 minutes with a clean interface that surfaces only what is relevant to current work
AI-powered triage automatically categorizes incoming issues, suggests assignees based on workload, and identifies stale items that need attention or closure
GitHub and GitLab integration creates branches from issues automatically and links pull requests to tickets, keeping engineering workflows unified without manual status updates
cons: Custom fields, workflows, and data models are rigid compared to Jira or Asana, frustrating teams with non-standard processes or regulatory tracking requirements
No built-in time tracking, resource management, or capacity planning, forcing engineering managers to use separate tools or spreadsheets for sprint planning
Free tier is limited to 10 users and 1 GB of attachment storage, making evaluation impractical for teams larger than a small startup without an immediate upgrade

content.title: Executive Summary
content.body: Linear is the fastest issue tracking and project management platform built specifically for software engineering teams. Its core design philosophy is speed — the web application delivers sub-100ms search across 10,000+ issues, instant filtering by any combination of status, assignee, label, priority, and cycle, and a keyboard-first interface with 200+ shortcuts that lets experienced engineers manage their entire workflow without touching a mouse. Linear was founded in 2019 by ex-Uber engineers and has grown rapidly through word-of-mouth adoption among high-performance engineering teams at companies like Vercel, Mercado Libre, and Podium. The platform uses a cycle-based planning model (similar to sprints but with more flexibility) and provides AI-powered issue triage that automatically categorizes, prioritizes, and assigns incoming work. Linear's simplicity is its defining characteristic — it makes deliberate trade-offs against Jira's configurability, choosing a fast, opinionated experience over unlimited customization. The platform has received over 3,500 user reviews with a 4.8/5 rating, reflecting exceptional satisfaction among its target audience of engineering teams.

content.title: Best For
content.body: Linear delivers the most value for software engineering teams of 5-50 people that prioritize speed and developer experience over process configurability. It is ideal for startups and growth-stage technology companies where engineering teams need to move fast and administrative overhead is a liability rather than a requirement. Product engineering teams that work in cycles (sprints) and need tight GitHub or GitLab integration for branch and pull request management benefit from Linear's automatic status synchronization. Teams migrating from Jira due to configuration complexity find Linear's opinionated simplicity liberating — after the initial adjustment period, most engineering teams report higher productivity and lower administrative burden. Organizations conducting AI-powered issue triage at scale benefit from Linear's automated labeling, prioritization, and assignment capabilities that reduce manual backlog grooming time. For engineering teams that value keyboard-driven workflows and fast, fluid interfaces, Linear provides the best experience available.

content.title: Worst Fit
content.body: Linear is a poor fit for three scenarios. Organizations requiring extensive customization — custom workflows with 10+ statuses, complex permission schemes with granular field-level controls, or non-engineering issue types like HR requests or legal reviews — will find Linear's data model too rigid. Teams in regulated industries (finance, healthcare, government) that need audit trails with approval gates, mandatory field validation, and separation-of-duties enforcement should use Jira, which provides the compliance infrastructure Linear deliberately omits. Organizations that need built-in time tracking, resource management, or capacity planning tools will need to supplement Linear with separate applications — engineering managers juggling spreadsheets for sprint planning while using Linear for issue tracking create the fragmentation Linear was designed to eliminate. Non-technical teams or enterprise organizations with diverse departmental needs will find Linear's engineering-focused feature set inadequate for marketing, sales, or operations workflows.

content.title: Key Features
content.body: Linear prioritizes developer experience through speed: the keyboard-first interface loads issues in under 100ms, the triage workflow ensures nothing falls through the cracks, and the GitHub sync creates bidirectional issue-PR links without third-party middleware.
items: Sub-100ms search across all issues with real-time filtering by status, assignee, label, priority, cycle, and any combination thereof, with zero pagination or loading states even on workspaces with 10,000+ issues.

Keyboard-first interface with 200+ customizable shortcuts enabling power users to navigate, create, triage, update, and close issues without leaving the keyboard, reducing context-switching overhead significantly.
AI issue triage automatically assigns labels, sets priority scores, suggests assignees based on team workload, and identifies stale issues needing closure or escalation based on historical patterns.

GitHub and GitLab integration auto-creates branches from Linear issues, links pull requests to tickets, and transitions issue statuses when PRs are merged — keeping development workflows synchronized without manual updates.

Cycle analytics track team velocity, cycle time distribution, issue acceptance rates, and throughput trends, providing data for retrospective discussions and sprint planning without separate reporting tools.
GraphQL API provides comprehensive programmatic access to all Linear data, enabling custom integrations, automation scripts, and reporting tools beyond the built-in capabilities.

content.title: Real Advantages
content.body: Linear's strongest advantage is raw performance. The application responds to every user interaction in under 100ms — issue creation, status changes, filtering, search queries — which creates a fluid experience that feels more like a native desktop app than a web application. This speed transforms daily workflow: engineers can triage 50+ issues in a morning standup without waiting for page loads, lag, or spinner states. The keyboard-first design is a genuine productivity multiplier for engineers who spend their day in terminal, IDE, and browser — eliminating mouse dependency for project management tasks reduces context-switching overhead. AI issue triage is another differentiator: rather than requiring manual labeling and prioritization, Linear's AI learns team patterns and automatically categorizes incoming issues with surprising accuracy after 2-3 cycles of training data. The GitHub/GitLab integration depth exceeds that of Jira or Asana — creating a branch directly from a Linear issue with the correct naming convention and linking the subsequent PR back to the issue creates a seamless code-to-task pipeline that reduces manual status updates.

content.title: Real Limitations
content.body: Linear's most significant limitation is its rigid data model and workflow structure. Unlike Jira, which lets organizations define custom statuses, fields, screens, and transitions for any process, Linear provides a fixed set of issue statuses (Triage, Backlog, Todo, In Progress, In Review, Done, Canceled) with limited customization options. Teams with non-standard workflows — such as multi-stage QA processes, legal review gates, or content publication pipelines — cannot model their processes within Linear without workarounds. The absence of built-in time tracking, resource management, and capacity planning tools forces engineering managers to maintain separate spreadsheets or adopt supplementary tools, creating exactly the tool fragmentation that Linear was designed to prevent. The free tier's restriction to 10 users makes evaluation difficult for teams of 11-50 people — they must commit to a paid plan before fully evaluating whether Linear meets their needs. Enterprise features like SAML SSO and audit logs require the Enterprise plan at $16/user/month, making Linear as expensive as Premium Jira for organizations that need these capabilities.

content.title: Pricing Explained
content.body: Linear uses per-user monthly pricing with three tiers. Free includes up to 10 users, 1 GB attachment storage, and core features with community support. Team plan at $8/user/month (billed annually) removes user and storage limits, adds cycle analytics, GitHub/GitLab integration, and Slack integration. Enterprise plan at $16/user/month adds SAML SSO, SCIM provisioning, advanced audit logs, API rate limit increases, and dedicated support with a 99.95% uptime SLA. All plans include the full set of core features — Linear does not gate issue tracking, search, or filtering behind higher tiers. The pricing model is straightforward with no hidden costs per seat, which is refreshing compared to Jira's tiered feature gating. However, the jump from Team to Enterprise doubles the per-user cost for access to SSO and audit logs, which are table-stakes features for many organizations. Annual billing is required for the published rates; monthly billing is available at approximately 20% higher per-user pricing. Linear does not charge for guest users or read-only viewers, which is advantageous for organizations with external contractors or stakeholders.

content.title: Hidden Costs
content.body: Linear's hidden costs are fewer than most project management tools but still relevant. Attachment storage on the Free plan (1 GB) fills quickly for teams sharing design files, screenshots, and logs; upgrading to Team removes the cap, but organizations with large file storage needs should evaluate whether Linear's attachment handling meets their requirements. Third-party integrations for missing capabilities — time tracking through Toggl or Harvest ($10-20/user/month), resource management through Float or Teamdeck ($6-10/user/month), and reporting through Mode or Metabase (free-$10/user/month) — add incremental costs that close the price gap with more feature-complete tools like Jira. The migration cost from Jira or Asana includes data mapping (issues with custom field values not present in Linear), workflow redesign (Jira's complex transitions simplified to Linear's linear flow), and team training on keyboard shortcuts and cycle-based planning. Most teams report a 4-8 week productivity dip during the transition as engineers adjust to Linear's different workflow model. API usage is rate-limited on Team plans, and organizations building custom integrations at scale may need to upgrade to Enterprise for higher rate limits.

content.title: Learning Curve
content.body: Basic proficiency — creating issues, using filters, managing cycles, and understanding the keyboard shortcut system — takes 2-4 hours for most engineers. Intermediate proficiency — mastering 50+ keyboard shortcuts, using the command menu (Cmd+K) for navigation, configuring automation rules, and building custom views — requires 1-2 weeks of regular use. Advanced proficiency — using the GraphQL API for custom tooling, implementing complex automation rules with multiple conditions, designing project structures for large workspaces, and optimizing cycle planning based on analytics — takes 3-6 weeks. Engineers migrating from Jira will experience initial frustration with Linear's limited customization but typically adapt faster than non-technical users. The primary learning challenge is unlearning Jira habits: Linear's cycle model differs from Jira's sprint model (cycles end on calendar dates rather than being manually started/stopped), and the absence of configurable workflows means teams adapt to Linear's process rather than adapting Linear to theirs. Linear's documentation is well-organized with video guides covering each feature set, and the keyboard shortcut reference is available directly within the app via the ? key.

content.title: Setup Time
content.body: A Linear workspace for a 10-person engineering team can be operational in 5-10 minutes: sign up, create a team, invite members, and start creating issues. There is no project configuration, scheme design, or workflow setup — Linear's opinionated defaults apply immediately. Importing existing issues from Jira, Asana, GitHub Issues, or Trello through Linear's import tools takes 30-60 minutes for up to 1,000 issues, including field mapping and attachment transfer. Configuring integrations — GitHub or GitLab repository linking, Slack notification routing, and webhook setup — takes 30-45 minutes per integration. Full organizational deployment for 50+ users with Enterprise features (SSO, SCIM, audit logs) and custom automation rules requires 1-2 days of configuration and testing. The fastest setup path among major project management tools is one of Linear's key selling points: there is no phase of 'setting up Jira' where administrators spend weeks configuring schemes before users can work. Teams can start using Linear productively on the same day they sign up, which significantly reduces the adoption friction common with enterprise project management tools.

content.title: Migration Difficulty
content.body: Migrating to Linear from another project management tool is moderately difficult, rated 5/10 on average but varying significantly by source platform. Migration from Jira is rated 7/10: Linear's import tool handles basic issue data (title, description, status, assignee, labels), but Jira-specific custom fields, time tracking entries, workflow history, comment timestamps, and plugin-dependent data do not transfer. The workflow redesign from Jira's complex transition model to Linear's linear status flow requires deliberate process simplification. Migration from Asana or Trello is rated 4/10: basic task data transfers cleanly, and the simpler data model of these tools means less data loss during import. Migration from GitHub Issues is rated 3/10: Linear's GitHub-native integration syncs issues bidirectionally, and the import handles labels, milestones, and assignee data completely. In all cases, a 2-week parallel run is recommended where both tools are active, allowing team members to transition at their own pace. The most significant migration challenge is change management: engineering teams accustomed to a specific workflow model need 2-4 weeks to adopt Linear's cycle-based approach. Historical data from the source tool remains accessible but is not integrated into Linear's search or analytics.

content.title: Integration Ecosystem
content.body: Linear's integration ecosystem is smaller than Jira's but more focused on engineering workflows. Direct integrations include GitHub (branch creation, PR linking, status sync), GitLab (merge request linking, branch creation), Slack (issue creation from messages, rich notifications with status updates), Figma (embedding designs in issue descriptions), Sentry (error tracking with automatic issue creation), and Raycast (quick issue search and creation from the Mac launcher). Linear's GraphQL API is comprehensive and well-documented, supporting all CRUD operations on issues, projects, cycles, teams, and users. The API's developer experience is notably good — GraphQL allows querying exactly the data needed in a single request, and the schema is self-documenting through GraphiQL. Linear does not offer a native marketplace or app directory for third-party extensions; all integrations beyond the direct partnerships require custom development through the API. Key missing integrations include native CI/CD tool connections beyond GitHub/GitLab (no Jenkins, CircleCI, or GitLab CI pipeline status in issues), native customer support integrations (Intercom, Zendesk), and native testing tool integrations. Organizations needing these capabilities must build custom connections via the API.

content.title: Security & Compliance
content.body: Linear is SOC 2 Type II certified and GDPR compliant. Data in transit uses TLS 1.2+ and data at rest is AES-256 encrypted. Enterprise plan customers get SAML 2.0 SSO with SCIM provisioning, advanced audit logs with 1-year retention, and a 99.95% uptime SLA. Linear stores data on Google Cloud Platform's US-based servers by default, with data residency options for EU customers on Enterprise plans. For compliance requirements: Linear provides a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) for GDPR compliance, supports data export for backup and migration purposes, and offers role-based access controls (admin, member, viewer) with granular permissions for project-level visibility. However, Linear lacks several enterprise compliance features that Jira provides: no HIPAA BAA coverage (making it unsuitable for healthcare software teams handling PHI), no FedRAMP authorization, no end-to-end encryption option for data at rest, no on-premise deployment option, and no field-level audit trails (audit logs track issue and project changes but not individual field edits). Organizations in regulated industries should verify that Linear's compliance posture meets their specific requirements before adoption.

content.title: Performance
content.body: Linear's performance is the best among all major project management tools, consistently earning 4.9/5 in user ratings. The web application delivers sub-100ms response times for all core operations — issue creation, status changes, filtering, search, and keyboard navigation — even on workspaces with 10,000+ issues and 50+ team members. The application uses optimistic UI updates: when a user changes an issue status, the interface updates immediately and syncs with the server in the background, eliminating the perception of latency. Search across all issues, projects, cycles, and users returns results in under 100ms with real-time filtering as the user types. The desktop application (Electron-based) consumes 100-200 MB of RAM, significantly less than Jira (300-500 MB in browser) or Asana (200-400 MB). The mobile app on iOS and Android opens in 2-3 seconds and provides full issue management capabilities, though the keyboard shortcut system does not translate to touch interfaces. Linear's uptime has been 99.99% since 2023 according to the platform status page, with no documented multi-hour outages. The only performance concern is initial load time for large workspaces with many teams and projects; Linear loads workspace metadata progressively, prioritizing the most recently accessed projects.

content.title: Customer Support
content.body: Linear provides email and in-app chat support across all paid plans. Free plan users have access to community forums and documentation. Team plan users receive email support with 12-24 hour response time during business days. Enterprise plan customers get priority email and chat support with 4-hour response SLA, a dedicated support engineer, and quarterly check-ins. Support quality is rated 4.5/5, reflecting the platform's focus on a responsive, helpful support experience that matches the product's quality. The documentation is clear, concise, and well-organized with written guides and short video tutorials covering every feature. Linear's changelog is actively maintained with monthly product updates, and feature requests are tracked in a public roadmap where users can vote on priorities. One limitation: Linear does not offer phone support at any tier, which may be a concern for organizations that require phone-based escalation for critical production issues. Enterprise users report positive experiences with their dedicated support contacts who provide guidance on workspace configuration, migration planning, and automation rule optimization. The community forum is relatively small compared to Jira's or Asana's but contains high-quality discussions with responses from the Linear team.

content.title: Real-world Use Cases
content.body: A 30-person SaaS engineering team uses Linear for all issue tracking, cycle planning, and code integration. Each 2-week cycle starts with a planning session where the team uses Linear's cycle view to review backlog priorities, estimate effort, and commit to issues based on historical velocity data from cycle analytics. Throughout the cycle, engineers create branches directly from Linear issues, and when they open PRs in GitHub, Linear automatically links the PR to the issue and transitions it to In Review. When the PR merges, the issue auto-transitions to Done. AI triage handles incoming bug reports from customer support by automatically categorizing, prioritizing, and assigning them to the appropriate team member based on historical assignment patterns. A 10-person startup uses Linear to run their entire product development process: product managers create feature requests as issues with priority scores, the engineering lead uses the roadmap view to plan quarterly initiatives, and individual contributors manage their daily work through the My Issues view filtered by status and cycle. Slack integration lets team members create issues from messages without switching contexts. A 100-person engineering organization uses Linear Enterprise with SAML SSO and audit logs to meet SOC 2 compliance requirements. They run 6 teams, each with separate projects and cycle schedules, coordinated through cross-project roadmap views.

content.title: Common Mistakes
content.body: Five mistakes repeatedly surface from Linear teams. Over-customizing before establishing a baseline — teams that create custom workflows, labels, and project structures during their first week risk building unnecessary complexity before understanding their natural workflow. Start with Linear's defaults for 2-3 cycles and adjust only when a clear process gap emerges. Ignoring keyboard shortcuts — engineers who use mouse-based navigation miss the primary productivity benefit of Linear. Investing 30 minutes learning the 30 most common shortcuts (n, c, e, 1-4, /, ?, ⌘K) pays dividends within the first week, but most new users never open the shortcut reference. Overcommitting cycles — treating Linear cycles as traditional sprint deadlines leads to scope creep and unplanned work. Linear's cycle model works best when teams commit to 60-70% of their capacity and leave buffer for bugs and unplanned work, tracked through the cycle analytics feature. Neglecting AI triage configuration — teams that disable or ignore AI issue triage miss a significant automation benefit. Spend 2-3 cycles training the AI with manual overrides, then enable auto-assignment with human review of automated decisions. Underusing the roadmap view — product managers who manage roadmaps outside Linear (in Notion, spreadsheets, or slide decks) create fragmentation. Linear's roadmap view supports project timelines and dependency tracking that eliminates duplicate status tracking.

content.title: Tips from experienced users
content.body: Power users rely on five strategies. Master the command menu (Cmd+K) — this is Linear's most powerful navigation tool, providing instant access to any command, project, issue, or user by typing 2-3 characters. Every action available through the interface is accessible through the command menu, making it the fastest way to work. Use saved views for recurring workflows — create custom views for 'bugs assigned to me this cycle', 'issues in Review by my team', or 'stale issues with no updates in 2 weeks' and bookmark them for daily use. Configure Slack integration to create issues from messages with @Linear — a slash command in Slack creates a Linear issue from a message thread, capturing context and conversation history automatically. Set up automation rules for common transitions — auto-assign issues based on label (UI bugs → frontend team, API issues → backend team), auto-transition to In Progress when a branch is created, and auto-close issues when linked PRs merge. Use cycle analytics during retrospectives — the cycle time distribution chart and throughput trend data provide objective data for process improvement discussions that replace subjective impressions. Review the changelog monthly; Linear ships updates rapidly, and new features often solve problems teams work around.

content.title: Alternatives
content.body: Linear's primary competitors address different needs on the project management spectrum. Jira offers comprehensive workflow customization, compliance features, and the Atlassian ecosystem, making it the right choice for regulated industries and large enterprise organizations at $7.75-15.25/user/month. Jira's flexibility is its strength, but its complexity is the reason most teams consider Linear. Asana provides a more intuitive project management experience for cross-functional teams with timeline views, goals, and workload management at $10.99-24.99/user/month, making it the better choice for organizations where engineering is one department among many. GitHub Projects offers native project management within the GitHub ecosystem for $4/user/month (GitHub Team), making it the most cost-effective and integrated choice for teams already using GitHub for code hosting. Notion combines project management with documentation, wikis, and databases at $8-15/user/month, appealing to teams that prefer all-in-one tools over best-in-class specialization. For each alternative, evaluate the performance cost: Linear's sub-100ms speed is unmatched; Jira and Asana feel sluggish by comparison, and the productivity difference of waiting 2-5 seconds per action accumulates to significant time savings over a year of daily use.

content.title: Final Verdict
content.body: Linear earns a 4.8/5 rating and is the best issue tracking tool available for software engineering teams that prioritize speed, developer experience, and simplicity over process configurability. Its sub-100ms search, keyboard-first design, and deep GitHub/GitLab integration create a workflow that respects engineers' time and attention. AI issue triage and cycle analytics provide genuine automation value that reduces administrative overhead. However, Linear is not the right choice for every organization. Teams that need complex custom workflows, enterprise compliance features (HIPAA, FedRAMP), built-in time tracking, or a tool that serves non-engineering departments should choose Jira or Asana instead. Linear's deliberate limitations are features for its target audience but dealbreakers for organizations with broader needs. For engineering-first teams of 5-50 people that value speed and simplicity, Linear is the clear best choice. The platform's rapid growth and exceptional user satisfaction ratings reflect a product that solved a real problem: project management software had become too complex for the teams it was supposed to serve. Linear proves that less configuration and more speed is a winning formula for high-performance engineering organizations.

faqs.question: What is Linear best used for?
faqs.answer: Linear is best used for software project management and issue tracking in engineering teams. It excels at managing sprints, tracking bugs and feature requests, and planning product roadmaps. Its deep version control integrations make it particularly valuable for development teams that use GitHub or GitLab.

faqs.question: How much does Linear cost?
faqs.answer: Linear offers a free tier limited to 10 users with basic features. The Team plan starts at $8/month per user and includes unlimited projects, cycles, and integrations. The Enterprise plan at $16/month per user adds SAML SSO, audit logs, and dedicated support with a 99.95% uptime SLA.

faqs.question: Does Linear integrate with GitHub?
faqs.answer: Yes, Linear offers deep GitHub integration that allows teams to link issues to branches, pull requests, and commits. When a PR is merged, the linked Linear issue can automatically transition to the Done state, keeping development workflows synchronized without manual updates.

faqs.question: Is Linear suitable for large teams?
faqs.answer: Yes, Linear supports large teams with its Enterprise plan that includes SAML SSO, advanced audit logging, and dedicated support. However, teams that require extensive customization or non-engineering workflows may find Linear's focused scope limiting compared to more general-purpose tools.
